title: Paths, files and text I/O

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific source-linked draft lesson for Paths, files and text I/O; independent technical, source, pedagogy, accessibility, and runnable-example review remains required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: Learn Java; accessed 2026-07; Exceptions and Java I/O learning paths; atomic topic: Paths, files and text I/O
sources.claim: Modern Java file code uses Path and Files with an explicit charset, bounded data handling, and deliberate policies for absence, replacement, partial writes, and path resolution.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Modern Java file code uses Path and Files with an explicit charset, bounded data handling, and deliberate policies for absence, replacement, partial writes, and path resolution.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A settings reader resolves a Path from configuration, reads UTF-8 text, validates every field, and distinguishes a missing file from malformed content. Your goal is to implement tested file persistence using modern path and file APIs. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Constructors and invariants; String and text processing; Checked and unchecked exception design. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language-specific operational model
sections.body: Modern Java file code uses Path and Files with an explicit charset, bounded data handling, and deliberate policies for absence, replacement, partial writes, and path resolution. This model is applied through the declared Java: Beginner to Intermediate behavior rather than a language-neutral slogan.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure model
sections.body: The working directory changes relative paths, text line endings vary, a write can fail partway, symbolic links and permissions matter, and Files convenience methods may load an entire file. The learner must reject this documented misconception: A relative filename always refers to the source-file directory, and Java text methods automatically choose the intended encoding.

includes: Read and write text with explicit charset, path and failure handling.
Implement tested file persistence using modern path and file APIs.
excludes: The working directory changes relative paths, text line endings vary, a write can fail partway, symbolic links and permissions matter, and Files convenience methods may load an entire file.

outcomes: Implement tested file persistence using modern path and file APIs.

examples.title: Paths, files and text I/O: direct language case
examples.situation: A settings reader resolves a Path from configuration, reads UTF-8 text, validates every field, and distinguishes a missing file from malformed content.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the concrete Java: Beginner to Intermediate state or tool result: A settings reader resolves a Path from configuration, reads UTF-8 text, validates every field, and distinguishes a missing file from malformed content.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the result with this correction: Resolve paths from an explicit base and specify the charset and replacement/failure policy; do not infer location from the source tree.
examples.result: The example meets the atomic outcome because Resolve paths from an explicit base and specify the charset and replacement/failure policy; do not infer location from the source tree.

examples.title: Paths, files and text I/O: boundary and transfer case
examples.situation: A save operation writes a sibling temporary file and then moves it according to a documented replacement policy instead of assuming one write is crash-atomic.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name what changed from the direct case: A save operation writes a sibling temporary file and then moves it according to a documented replacement policy instead of assuming one write is crash-atomic.

Step 2 — Keep the language boundary explicit: The working directory changes relative paths, text line endings vary, a write can fail partway, symbolic links and permissions matter, and Files convenience methods may load an entire file.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting but incorrect rule: A relative filename always refers to the source-file directory, and Java text methods automatically choose the intended encoding.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Implement tested file persistence using modern path and file APIs.

misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Predict both examples and identify the first observable contradiction in this belief: A relative filename always refers to the source-file directory, and Java text methods automatically choose the intended encoding.

analogy.example: a document desk that records a failure chain, handles only recoverable cases, and always closes borrowed equipment can introduce Paths, files and text I/O, after the canonical Java: Beginner to Intermediate rule is stated first.
usefulFor: exception boundaries, paths, text encodings, and resource cleanup
limitations: suppressed exceptions, stack unwinding, filesystem semantics, and serialization attacks need exact technical treatment
